title: The Speed-of-Light Tax
scenario: A user in Tokyo sends a request to a datacenter in Virginia. The fiber-optic path is approximately 10,000 km. What is the minimum possible one-way latency for this request, assuming light travels at 200,000 km/s in fiber?

details.common_mistake: Using the speed of light in vacuum (300,000 km/s) instead of the speed of light in fiber (~200,000 km/s, roughly 2/3 c). This underestimates latency by 50%.
details.realistic_solution: The minimum one-way latency is 50ms. Light in fiber travels at ~200,000 km/s, so 10,000 km / 200,000 km/s = 50ms. The round-trip time (RTT) would be ~100ms. This is a hard physics floor — no amount of engineering can reduce it below this for a given fiber path.
details.napkin_math: One-way latency = distance / speed = 10,000 km / 200,000 km/s = 0.05s = 50ms. RTT = 2 × 50ms = 100ms. This is the theoretical minimum; real-world RTT is typically 120-150ms due to routing, switching, and amplification hops.

title: The Datacenter Overhead Multiplier
scenario: A hyperscaler reports a PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) of 1.2 for their datacenter. If the IT equipment draws 10 MW of power, how much total power does the facility consume?

details.common_mistake: Thinking PUE is a percentage overhead. A PUE of 1.2 does not mean 1.2% overhead — it means the facility draws 1.2× the IT load, so 20% goes to cooling, lighting, and other non-compute overhead.
details.realistic_solution: Total facility power = PUE × IT load = 1.2 × 10 MW = 12 MW. The extra 2 MW goes to cooling (the dominant factor), power distribution losses, lighting, and security systems. Industry-leading datacenters achieve PUE ~1.1, while older facilities may have PUE of 1.5-2.0.
details.napkin_math: Total power = PUE × IT power = 1.2 × 10 MW = 12 MW. Overhead = 12 MW - 10 MW = 2 MW wasted. At $0.05/kWh, that overhead costs 2,000 kW × 8,760 hours × $0.05 = $876,000/year just for cooling and infrastructure.

title: The Intercontinental Bottleneck
scenario: A company wants to do distributed training across datacenters in the US and Europe. The submarine cable connecting them has a capacity of 200 Tbps shared among thousands of users. Why is cross-Atlantic distributed training fundamentally different from intra-datacenter training?

details.common_mistake: Focusing only on bandwidth and ignoring latency. Even with 200 Tbps total cable capacity, the ~60ms RTT US-Europe latency creates a synchronization barrier that dwarfs the ~1μs intra-datacenter latency by 60,000×.
details.realistic_solution: Two fundamental barriers: (1) Latency: US-Europe RTT is ~60ms vs ~1μs within a datacenter (60,000× difference). Every gradient synchronization step in data-parallel training pays this penalty. (2) Bandwidth per tenant: The 200 Tbps is shared capacity. A single tenant might get 10-100 Gbps — far below the 3.2 Tbps NVLink or 400 Gbps InfiniBand available within a datacenter. Together, these make synchronous distributed training across continents impractical for most workloads.
details.napkin_math: Intra-DC latency: ~1μs. Cross-Atlantic latency: ~60ms. Ratio: 60,000×. If AllReduce on 8 GPUs in one rack takes 1ms, the equivalent cross-Atlantic AllReduce takes 60ms+ (dominated by RTT, not bandwidth). For a training step that does 50ms of compute, intra-DC overhead is 2% but cross-Atlantic overhead is 55%.

title: The Data Residency Constraint
scenario: Your company serves EU customers and stores their personal data for ML model training. Under GDPR, what is 'data residency' and why does it constrain where you can place your training infrastructure?

details.common_mistake: Thinking GDPR only applies to EU-headquartered companies. GDPR applies to any organization processing data of EU residents, regardless of where the company is based. Moving training data to a US datacenter without proper safeguards violates the regulation.
details.realistic_solution: Data residency means personal data must be stored and processed within approved jurisdictions (typically the EU/EEA). GDPR restricts transferring personal data outside the EU unless the destination country has an 'adequacy decision' or the transfer uses approved mechanisms (Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules). This means ML training pipelines must either: (1) run in EU datacenters, (2) use anonymized/pseudonymized data that no longer qualifies as personal data, or (3) implement approved cross-border transfer mechanisms. Violations can result in fines up to 4% of global annual revenue.
details.napkin_math: Fine risk: 4% of global revenue. For a $10B company, that is $400M maximum fine. Cost of EU datacenter region: ~10-20% premium over cheapest US regions. The compliance cost is trivially justified by the fine risk.

title: The Grid Emissions Gap
scenario: France's electricity grid produces ~50 gCO2/kWh (mostly nuclear), while Poland's grid produces ~700 gCO2/kWh (mostly coal). If you run the same 1,000 GPU-hour training job in each country, how does the carbon footprint differ?

details.common_mistake: Assuming carbon footprint is only about energy efficiency or PUE. A perfectly efficient datacenter on a coal-heavy grid emits far more CO2 than a less efficient one on a nuclear/renewable grid. The grid carbon intensity dominates.
details.realistic_solution: France: 1,000 GPU-hours × 0.3 kW (A100) × 50 gCO2/kWh = 15,000 gCO2 = 15 kg CO2. Poland: 1,000 GPU-hours × 0.3 kW × 700 gCO2/kWh = 210,000 gCO2 = 210 kg CO2. Running in Poland produces 14× more CO2 for identical compute. This is why carbon-aware scheduling — shifting workloads to low-carbon grids — is the single highest-leverage sustainability intervention.
details.napkin_math: France: 1,000 h × 300W × 50 gCO2/kWh = 1,000 × 0.3 kWh × 50 = 15,000 g = 15 kg CO2. Poland: 1,000 × 0.3 × 700 = 210,000 g = 210 kg CO2. Ratio: 210/15 = 14×. One transatlantic flight ≈ 1,000 kg CO2, so the Poland training run is ~21% of a flight.

title: The Edge Cache Inference Pattern
scenario: A company deploys a text classification model that maps product reviews to 5 sentiment categories. They receive 10 million reviews per day, but only 50,000 unique review texts. What serving architecture exploits this redundancy?

details.common_mistake: Running inference on every single request. With 10M requests but only 50K unique inputs, 99.5% of requests are redundant inference — wasting GPU compute on inputs already seen.
details.realistic_solution: Use a CDN-style caching layer in front of the inference service. Hash each input text and check a distributed cache (Redis, Memcached, or literal CDN edge nodes). On cache hit, return the cached prediction in <1ms. On cache miss, run inference and cache the result. With 50K unique inputs out of 10M requests, the cache hit rate is 99.5%. This reduces GPU inference load by 200× — from 10M to 50K inferences per day.
details.napkin_math: Without caching: 10M inferences/day. With caching: 50K inferences/day (cache miss only). Reduction: 10M/50K = 200×. If each inference costs $0.001, daily cost drops from $10,000 to $50. Cache storage: 50K entries × 1KB each = 50MB — trivial.

title: The GPU Cluster Power Wall
scenario: You want to build a 10,000 H100 GPU training cluster. Each H100 consumes 700W at peak. What is the total power draw, and why is securing this amount of power a major infrastructure challenge?

details.common_mistake: Only counting GPU power and forgetting PUE overhead, networking, CPUs, storage, and cooling. Also underestimating how difficult it is to secure multi-MW power contracts.
details.realistic_solution: GPU power alone: 10,000 × 700W = 7 MW. With PUE of 1.3 (cooling, networking, storage, power distribution): 7 MW × 1.3 = 9.1 MW total facility draw. This is equivalent to powering ~7,000 US homes. Securing 9+ MW of reliable power requires: (1) proximity to high-voltage transmission lines, (2) long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs), (3) often 12-24 months of lead time for grid connection. Power availability is now the primary bottleneck for new GPU clusters, not chip supply.
details.napkin_math: GPU power: 10,000 × 700W = 7,000 kW = 7 MW. With PUE 1.3: 7 × 1.3 = 9.1 MW. Annual energy: 9.1 MW × 8,760 h = 79,716 MWh. At $0.05/kWh: $3.99M/year in electricity alone. Average US home: ~1.3 kW → 9,100 kW / 1.3 = ~7,000 homes equivalent.

title: The Risk Classification Framework
scenario: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems into four risk tiers: unacceptable, high-risk, limited-risk, and minimal-risk. A company deploys an LLM-based chatbot for customer service in the EU. Which risk tier does it fall under, and what are the compliance obligations?

details.common_mistake: Assuming all AI systems are 'high-risk' under the AI Act. Most general-purpose chatbots fall under 'limited-risk,' which requires transparency obligations (telling users they are interacting with AI) but not the extensive conformity assessments required for high-risk systems like medical diagnosis or criminal justice.
details.realistic_solution: A general customer-service chatbot is 'limited-risk' under the AI Act. The primary obligation is transparency: users must be informed they are interacting with an AI system. However, if the chatbot is used in a high-risk domain (e.g., insurance claim decisions, employment screening), it escalates to 'high-risk' — requiring conformity assessments, technical documentation, human oversight mechanisms, and registration in the EU database. The classification depends on the USE CASE, not the technology.
details.napkin_math: Compliance cost estimate: Limited-risk transparency: ~$10K (disclosure UI + documentation). High-risk conformity assessment: $200K-$500K (audit, documentation, monitoring systems, human oversight tooling). Non-compliance fine: up to €35M or 7% of global revenue, whichever is higher.

title: The Gradient Staleness Problem
scenario: In asynchronous distributed training across two datacenters with 60ms RTT, Worker B applies a gradient update that was computed 3 steps ago (before Workers A and C had pushed their updates). What is this phenomenon called, and why is it harmful?

details.common_mistake: Thinking asynchronous training is always better because it avoids synchronization barriers. Gradient staleness means workers optimize against an outdated model, which can cause training divergence or significantly slower convergence.
details.realistic_solution: This is 'gradient staleness' — a gradient computed at step t being applied at step t+k (here k=3). The gradient points in the direction that was correct 3 steps ago, but the model has since moved. The harm scales with staleness: (1) Mild staleness (k=1-2): slightly slower convergence, usually tolerable. (2) Moderate staleness (k=3-10): requires learning rate reduction proportional to staleness, slowing training. (3) Severe staleness (k>10): can cause training divergence. Mitigation strategies include staleness-weighted updates, bounded staleness protocols (wait if staleness > threshold), or switching to synchronous training with gradient compression.
details.napkin_math: Cross-DC RTT: 60ms. Training step duration: 20ms. In 60ms (1 RTT), the local worker completes 3 steps. So any gradient sent from the remote DC arrives with staleness k=3. Effective learning rate reduction needed: ~1/(1+k) = 1/4 = 25% of original LR, slowing convergence by ~4×.

title: The Anycast Routing Pattern
scenario: A global ML inference service has endpoints in US-East, EU-West, and Asia-Pacific. A user in Berlin sends a request. With anycast routing, how does the network decide which datacenter handles the request?

details.common_mistake: Thinking the application or a load balancer decides the routing. With anycast, the BGP routing protocol at the network layer automatically routes packets to the nearest (in network hops) datacenter advertising the same IP address — no application-level routing logic needed.
details.realistic_solution: Anycast assigns the same IP address to all three datacenter endpoints. When the Berlin user's request enters the internet, BGP routers along the path forward it to the topologically nearest datacenter — in this case EU-West. This happens at the network layer, below the application. Benefits: (1) Automatic failover — if EU-West goes down, BGP reconverges and traffic shifts to the next-nearest DC. (2) Minimal latency — requests are served by the closest DC by default. (3) DDoS resilience — attack traffic is distributed across all DCs. Limitation: anycast routes by network topology, not by server load — a hot DC still gets all nearby traffic.
details.napkin_math: Berlin → EU-West: ~10ms RTT. Berlin → US-East: ~90ms RTT. Berlin → Asia-Pacific: ~200ms RTT. Anycast automatically picks the 10ms path. Latency improvement vs random routing: up to 20× (200ms/10ms worst-to-best case).

title: The Gradient Sync Penalty
scenario: You are training a 10B parameter model with data parallelism across 2 datacenters: 64 GPUs in US-East and 64 GPUs in EU-West, connected by a 100 Gbps link with 60ms RTT. Each gradient is FP32 (4 bytes per parameter). Calculate the time for a single cross-datacenter AllReduce of the full gradient tensor.

details.common_mistake: Only calculating bandwidth time and ignoring latency. At 60ms RTT, the latency component alone exceeds many intra-datacenter AllReduce operations. Also forgetting that ring AllReduce requires 2(n-1)/n passes of the data.
details.realistic_solution: Gradient size: 10B × 4 bytes = 40 GB. For a 2-node (2-DC) AllReduce, the data transferred across the link is 2 × (n-1)/n × 40 GB = 2 × 1/2 × 40 GB = 40 GB (reduce-scatter + all-gather, each sending half). Bandwidth time: 40 GB / (100 Gbps / 8) = 40 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 3.2 seconds. Latency overhead: ring AllReduce with 2 nodes needs 2 phases × 60ms = 120ms (negligible vs bandwidth). Total: ~3.2 seconds per AllReduce. If the compute per step is 2 seconds, the communication overhead is 62% — making cross-DC synchronous training severely inefficient.
details.napkin_math: Gradient: 10B params × 4 B = 40 GB. Cross-DC bandwidth: 100 Gbps = 12.5 GB/s. Transfer for 2-node AllReduce: 40 GB (reduce-scatter 20 GB + all-gather 20 GB). Time = 40 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 3.2s. Latency overhead: 2 × 60ms = 0.12s. Total: ~3.3s. Compute per step: ~2s. Communication-to-compute ratio: 3.3/2 = 1.65. Efficiency: 2/(2+3.3) = 37.7%.

title: The CO2 Per Training Run
scenario: You train a model using 256 A100 GPUs (300W each) for 14 days in a datacenter with PUE 1.3, located in a region with grid carbon intensity of 400 gCO2/kWh. Calculate the total CO2 emissions of this training run in metric tons.

details.common_mistake: Forgetting to include PUE in the energy calculation. Without PUE, you underestimate energy by 30%. Also, mixing up grams and kilograms — the final answer should be in metric tons (1,000 kg) for readability.
details.realistic_solution: Step 1: GPU energy = 256 GPUs × 0.3 kW × 24 h × 14 days = 25,804.8 kWh. Step 2: Total energy with PUE = 25,804.8 × 1.3 = 33,546.2 kWh. Step 3: CO2 = 33,546.2 kWh × 400 gCO2/kWh = 13,418,496 g = 13.4 metric tons CO2. For reference, the average American's annual carbon footprint is ~16 metric tons, so this single training run produces nearly a year's worth of one person's emissions.
details.napkin_math: Energy = 256 × 0.3 kW × 336 h = 25,805 kWh. With PUE 1.3: 33,546 kWh. CO2 = 33,546 × 400 g/kWh = 13,418,400 g ≈ 13.4 metric tons. Comparison: ~1 person-year of US emissions. If run in France (50 gCO2/kWh instead): 33,546 × 50 = 1,677,300 g = 1.7 tons — an 8× reduction just by choosing the right region.

title: The SLA Decomposition Problem
scenario: Your global inference service has a 200ms P99 SLA. A user in Sydney, Australia hits your nearest datacenter in Singapore (~3,400 km away). Network RTT is 55ms. Model inference takes 80ms. TLS handshake adds 1 RTT. Remaining processing (load balancer, pre/post-processing) takes 15ms. Does this request meet the SLA? Show your latency budget breakdown.

details.common_mistake: Forgetting that TLS adds a full extra RTT on top of the data RTT. The first connection requires a TCP handshake (1 RTT) + TLS handshake (1 RTT) + data transfer (1 RTT) = 3 RTTs minimum without connection reuse.
details.realistic_solution: Latency budget (first request, no connection reuse): TCP handshake: 55ms (1 RTT). TLS handshake: 55ms (1 RTT). Request send + inference + response: 55ms (1 RTT for data) + 80ms (inference) + 15ms (processing). Total: 55 + 55 + 55 + 80 + 15 = 260ms. This EXCEEDS the 200ms SLA by 60ms. Mitigation: use TLS session resumption (saves 1 RTT = 55ms → 205ms), or use HTTP/2 with persistent connections (amortizes handshake across requests → steady-state 150ms). Also consider deploying a model replica in Australia to eliminate the 55ms per-RTT overhead entirely.
details.napkin_math: Cold request: TCP(55) + TLS(55) + Data RTT(55) + Inference(80) + Processing(15) = 260ms > 200ms SLA. FAIL. With TLS resumption: TCP(55) + TLS-resume(0) + Data(55) + Inference(80) + Processing(15) = 205ms. Still over by 5ms. With persistent connection: Data(55) + Inference(80) + Processing(15) = 150ms. PASS with 50ms headroom.

title: The Electricity Arbitrage
scenario: Your 1,000-GPU H100 cluster (700W each, PUE 1.25) runs batch training jobs that can tolerate 6-hour scheduling delays. Electricity prices in your region fluctuate: $0.03/kWh at night (11pm-7am) and $0.12/kWh during peak (11am-5pm). Calculate the annual savings of scheduling all batch jobs during off-peak hours.

details.common_mistake: Calculating savings only on GPU power and forgetting PUE. Also assuming 24/7 utilization when the question specifies batch jobs that can be shifted to 8 off-peak hours.
details.realistic_solution: Cluster power with PUE: 1,000 × 700W × 1.25 = 875 kW. If batch jobs fill 8 hours/day of off-peak: Energy per day = 875 kW × 8h = 7,000 kWh. Off-peak cost: 7,000 × $0.03 = $210/day. Peak cost (same work): 7,000 × $0.12 = $840/day. Daily savings: $840 - $210 = $630. Annual savings: $630 × 365 = $229,950. This is ~$230K/year saved purely by time-shifting deferrable workloads — no hardware changes needed.
details.napkin_math: Power: 1,000 × 0.7 kW × 1.25 PUE = 875 kW. Daily energy (8h): 875 × 8 = 7,000 kWh. Peak price: 7,000 × $0.12 = $840. Off-peak price: 7,000 × $0.03 = $210. Savings: $630/day × 365 = $229,950/year. As % of annual electricity: savings / peak cost = $230K / $307K = 75% reduction for time-shifted workloads.

title: The Gradient Compression Dividend
scenario: Your cross-DC AllReduce for a 10B parameter model takes 3.2 seconds over a 100 Gbps link (40 GB of FP32 gradients). You apply Top-K gradient sparsification that transmits only the top 1% of gradient values (plus their indices, encoded as 4-byte ints). Calculate the new AllReduce time and the effective compression ratio.

details.common_mistake: Forgetting that sparse gradients require transmitting both values AND indices. The index overhead means 1% sparsity does not yield 100× compression — it yields ~50× because each selected element needs 8 bytes (4B value + 4B index) instead of 4B.
details.realistic_solution: Original: 10B params × 4B = 40 GB. With Top-1% sparsification: selected elements = 10B × 0.01 = 100M elements. Each element needs: 4B (FP32 value) + 4B (int32 index) = 8B. Compressed size: 100M × 8B = 800 MB. Compression ratio: 40 GB / 0.8 GB = 50×. New transfer time: 0.8 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 64ms. Plus latency: 120ms. Total: ~184ms vs original 3.3s — an 18× speedup. However, Top-K sparsification introduces approximation error that may require more training steps to converge.
details.napkin_math: Original: 40 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 3.2s + 0.12s latency = 3.32s. Compressed: 100M elements × 8B = 800 MB. Transfer: 800 MB / 12,500 MB/s = 64ms. Total: 64ms + 120ms = 184ms. Speedup: 3,320ms / 184ms = 18×. Compression ratio: 40,000 MB / 800 MB = 50×. Note: 50× not 100× because of index overhead.

title: The Model Sync Cost
scenario: You deploy a 70B parameter model (FP16, so 140 GB) to 5 global regions for low-latency inference. After each fine-tuning run, you must push the updated weights to all regions over 10 Gbps inter-region links. How long does a full model sync take, and what is the monthly egress cost at $0.08/GB?

details.common_mistake: Assuming you must push the full 140 GB to each region sequentially. A tree-based distribution (origin → 2 regions → 2 more) or delta-based sync (only changed layers) can dramatically reduce total sync time.
details.realistic_solution: Sequential push to 5 regions: 140 GB × 5 = 700 GB total egress. Transfer time per region: 140 GB / (10 Gbps / 8) = 140 / 1.25 = 112 seconds. Sequential total: 112s × 5 = 560s = 9.3 minutes. With parallel push (origin has enough uplink): 112 seconds (all 5 in parallel, but origin needs 50 Gbps uplink). Monthly cost (assuming 2 syncs/week): 700 GB × 8 syncs/month = 5,600 GB × $0.08 = $448/month. Optimization: use LoRA adapters (only sync the 0.1% of parameters that changed) — reduces sync payload from 140 GB to ~140 MB, cutting time to 0.1s and cost to $0.45/month.
details.napkin_math: Model size: 70B × 2B = 140 GB. Per-region transfer: 140 GB / 1.25 GB/s = 112s. Total egress for 5 regions: 140 × 5 = 700 GB. Monthly (2×/week): 700 × 8 = 5,600 GB. Cost: 5,600 × $0.08 = $448/month. With LoRA delta sync (0.1%): 140 MB × 5 × 8 = 5.6 GB → $0.45/month. Savings: 99.9%.

title: The Async Pipeline Throughput Limit
scenario: You split a 48-layer model across 2 datacenters using pipeline parallelism: layers 1-24 in US-East, layers 25-48 in EU-West. The cross-DC link has 40ms one-way latency. Each pipeline stage takes 50ms of compute. With 4 microbatches in flight, calculate the pipeline bubble and steady-state throughput.

details.common_mistake: Ignoring the communication latency between pipeline stages. Intra-datacenter pipeline parallelism has ~microsecond inter-stage latency; cross-DC has 40ms, which fundamentally changes the pipeline bubble calculation.
details.realistic_solution: In pipeline parallelism, activations must be sent from stage 1 (US-East) to stage 2 (EU-West) after each microbatch's forward pass, and gradients sent back during backward. Effective stage time = compute + communication = 50ms + 40ms = 90ms per stage transition. With 2 stages and 4 microbatches: pipeline fill time = (2-1) × 90ms = 90ms. Steady-state: 1 microbatch completes every 90ms (bottleneck is the stage time including communication). Total time for 4 microbatches: 90ms (fill) + 4 × 90ms (steady state) = 450ms. Without cross-DC latency: (1 × 50ms) + 4 × 50ms = 250ms. Cross-DC overhead: 450/250 = 1.8× slowdown.
details.napkin_math: Stage time with comms: 50ms compute + 40ms network = 90ms. Pipeline fill (p-1 stages): 1 × 90ms = 90ms. 4 microbatches at steady state: 4 × 90ms = 360ms. Total: 450ms. Without network: 1 × 50ms + 4 × 50ms = 250ms. Slowdown: 450/250 = 1.8×. Bubble fraction: 90/(90 + 4×90) = 20%. Compute efficiency: 50/90 = 55.6% (44.4% wasted on network wait).

title: The Right-to-Be-Forgotten Cost
scenario: Under GDPR, a user requests deletion of their data. Your model was trained on a dataset including 10M users' data over 2,048 A100 GPU-hours. If you must retrain from scratch (excluding the user's data), what is the compute cost? If retraining costs $2/GPU-hour, what does each deletion request cost?

details.common_mistake: Assuming you can simply delete the user's data from the database and call it done. If the model has memorized patterns from that user's data, the model weights themselves may encode personal information. Full compliance may require retraining or machine unlearning techniques.
details.realistic_solution: Full retrain cost: 2,048 GPU-hours × $2/GPU-hour = $4,096 per deletion request. At 100 deletion requests/year, that is $409,600/year in retraining costs. This is why organizations invest in: (1) Machine unlearning: approximate methods that update model weights to 'forget' specific training examples without full retraining (cost: ~1-5% of retraining). (2) Training data isolation: shard training data by user cohort so retraining only requires processing the affected shard. (3) Differential privacy: train with DP guarantees so no individual's data is memorized — making deletion requests moot for the model.
details.napkin_math: Full retrain: 2,048 GPU-h × $2 = $4,096/request. 100 requests/year: $409,600. Machine unlearning (1% of retrain cost): $40.96/request → $4,096/year. Savings: $405,504/year. Break-even: unlearning R&D costs ~$200K → pays for itself in 6 months at 100 deletions/year. DP training overhead: ~10-30% more training time upfront but $0/deletion thereafter.

title: The Follow-the-Sun Savings
scenario: You have GPU clusters in 3 regions. Region A (solar-heavy grid): 100 gCO2/kWh during day, 500 gCO2/kWh at night. Region B (wind-heavy): 200 gCO2/kWh always. Region C (coal baseline): 600 gCO2/kWh always. A 48-hour training job requires 500 kW continuously. Calculate CO2 for: (a) running entirely in Region C, and (b) a 'follow-the-renewables' schedule that uses Region A during daytime (12h) and Region B at night (12h).

details.common_mistake: Assuming carbon-aware scheduling always uses the single greenest region. The greenest region changes throughout the day as renewable generation fluctuates. Static assignment to one 'green' region misses the dynamic optimization opportunity.
details.realistic_solution: (a) Region C only: 500 kW × 48h × 600 gCO2/kWh = 14,400,000 g = 14.4 metric tons CO2. (b) Follow-the-renewables: Day in Region A (24h total across 2 days): 500 kW × 24h × 100 gCO2/kWh = 1,200,000 g. Night in Region B (24h total): 500 kW × 24h × 200 gCO2/kWh = 2,400,000 g. Total: 3,600,000 g = 3.6 metric tons CO2. Reduction: 14.4 - 3.6 = 10.8 tons saved = 75% reduction. The cost: cross-region migration overhead (checkpoint, transfer, restart) adds ~30 minutes per migration = 2 hours overhead for 4 migrations, a 4.2% time penalty.
details.napkin_math: (a) Static (Region C): 500 × 48 × 600 = 14,400,000 g = 14.4t. (b) Dynamic: Day@A: 500 × 24 × 100 = 1,200,000g. Night@B: 500 × 24 × 200 = 2,400,000g. Total = 3,600,000g = 3.6t. Savings: (14.4 - 3.6) / 14.4 = 75%. Migration cost: 4 migrations × 30min = 2h overhead on 48h job = 4.2% time penalty for 75% carbon reduction.

title: The Time-to-First-Token Across Oceans
scenario: An LLM inference service in US-East has a prefill time of 120ms for a 500-token prompt. A user in London (RTT = 75ms) and a user in Tokyo (RTT = 160ms) each send the same prompt. Assuming persistent connections (no TLS overhead), calculate the time-to-first-token (TTFT) experienced by each user. The service also has a 200ms TTFT SLA.

details.common_mistake: Thinking TTFT = prefill time. TTFT as experienced by the user includes the network round trip: request travel time + prefill + first token travel time = 1 RTT + prefill.
details.realistic_solution: User-perceived TTFT = request one-way latency + prefill + response one-way latency = RTT + prefill (since one-way ≈ RTT/2 each way). London: 75ms + 120ms = 195ms. Meets 200ms SLA (barely, 5ms margin). Tokyo: 160ms + 120ms = 280ms. Violates 200ms SLA by 80ms. To fix Tokyo, options: (1) Deploy model replica in Asia-Pacific (TTFT = ~10ms + 120ms = 130ms). (2) Use speculative decoding to reduce prefill to 60ms (TTFT = 160 + 60 = 220ms — still over). (3) Cache common prompt prefixes at edge (if applicable). The physics-based analysis makes it clear: only deploying a regional replica solves the Tokyo SLA.
details.napkin_math: London TTFT: 75ms RTT + 120ms prefill = 195ms ≤ 200ms SLA. PASS. Tokyo TTFT: 160ms RTT + 120ms prefill = 280ms > 200ms SLA. FAIL by 80ms. Required prefill for Tokyo to meet SLA: 200 - 160 = 40ms (3× faster than current — unlikely without model change). Regional replica in Tokyo: 10ms RTT + 120ms = 130ms. Cost: ~$50K/month for H100 inference cluster.

title: The Sync vs Async Training Dilemma
scenario: Your company has 512 H100 GPUs split across US-East (256) and US-West (256), connected by a 400 Gbps link with 40ms RTT. You need to train a 30B parameter model. The team is debating: (A) synchronous data-parallel training across both DCs, (B) fully asynchronous training, or (C) training two separate models and merging. Evaluate each approach and recommend one.

details.common_mistake: Choosing synchronous training because it is the standard approach. At 40ms RTT with 30B parameters, the AllReduce overhead makes synchronous cross-DC training extremely inefficient, but async training introduces convergence risks that junior engineers underestimate.
details.realistic_solution: Analysis of each approach: (A) Synchronous: Gradient size = 30B × 4B = 120 GB. AllReduce across 2 DCs: 120 GB / 50 GB/s (400Gbps/8) = 2.4s per step. If compute per step = 1.5s, efficiency = 1.5/(1.5+2.4) = 38%. Terrible utilization. (B) Asynchronous: Each DC trains independently but pushes gradient updates to the other. Staleness = ~3 steps at 40ms RTT. Requires learning rate reduction (~0.5×), so effective convergence speed is 2 DCs × 0.5 = equivalent to 1 DC. No benefit over using just one DC. (C) Separate training + model merging (e.g., DiLoCo / federated approach): Each DC trains independently for K steps on its own data shard, then exchanges only model deltas every K steps. With K=500, communication happens once every ~12 minutes, making the 40ms RTT negligible. Recent research (DiLoCo, Streaming DiLoCo) shows this achieves 90-95% of synchronous quality. Recommendation: Option C — federated/DiLoCo-style with periodic synchronization every 500 steps.
details.napkin_math: (A) Sync: AllReduce = 120GB / 50GB/s = 2.4s. Compute = 1.5s. Efficiency = 38%. (B) Async: staleness k=3 → LR reduction 0.5× → net throughput gain = 2×0.5 = 1.0× (no gain). (C) DiLoCo (K=500 steps): Communication every 500 × 1.5s = 750s. Sync cost per exchange: 120GB / 50GB/s = 2.4s. Overhead: 2.4/750 = 0.32%. Efficiency: ~99.7%. Quality: ~93% of fully sync. Winner: (C) by a wide margin.

title: The Latency-Carbon-Cost Triangle
scenario: You must deploy an LLM inference service for users in Europe. Three candidate regions: (A) Frankfurt — 15ms to users, $0.10/kWh electricity, 350 gCO2/kWh (German grid). (B) Stockholm — 35ms to users, $0.04/kWh, 30 gCO2/kWh (Swedish hydro). (C) Paris — 25ms to users, $0.07/kWh, 55 gCO2/kWh (French nuclear). Your SLA is 500ms TTFT and model prefill is 400ms. Evaluate the tradeoffs and recommend a deployment strategy.

details.common_mistake: Picking the lowest-latency option (Frankfurt) without evaluating whether the SLA can be met from other regions. Since prefill = 400ms and SLA = 500ms, any region with RTT < 100ms works — all three qualify. Once the SLA constraint is satisfied, the decision should optimize on cost and carbon.
details.realistic_solution: SLA check: Required RTT < 500ms - 400ms = 100ms. All three regions qualify: Frankfurt (15ms), Paris (25ms), Stockholm (35ms). Since all meet the SLA, the tie-breaker is cost and carbon: Frankfurt: $0.10/kWh, 350 gCO2/kWh — most expensive, highest carbon. Paris: $0.07/kWh, 55 gCO2/kWh — moderate cost, low carbon. Stockholm: $0.04/kWh, 30 gCO2/kWh — cheapest, lowest carbon, but highest latency (still well within SLA). Recommendation: Stockholm as primary (cheapest, greenest, 435ms TTFT well within 500ms SLA). Paris as failover (moderate cost/carbon, 425ms TTFT). Frankfurt only if Stockholm + Paris both fail. For a 100-GPU H100 cluster running 24/7: Stockholm annual electricity = 100 × 0.7kW × 8,760h × $0.04 = $24,528. Frankfurt: $61,320. Savings: $36,792/year plus 10× lower carbon.
details.napkin_math: SLA budget for network: 500ms - 400ms = 100ms. All pass. Annual electricity (100 H100s, PUE 1.2): Frankfurt: 100×0.7×1.2×8760×$0.10 = $73,584. Paris: ×$0.07 = $51,509. Stockholm: ×$0.04 = $29,434. Carbon (annual): Frankfurt: 73,584 kWh × 350g = 25.75t. Paris: 51,509 × 55g = 2.83t. Stockholm: 29,434 × 30g = 0.88t. Stockholm wins on both cost (-60% vs Frankfurt) and carbon (-97% vs Frankfurt).

title: The Data Locality Penalty
scenario: A healthcare company must train a diagnostic model on patient data from 3 countries: US (60% of data, 500K records), Germany (25%, 210K), Japan (15%, 125K). HIPAA (US), GDPR (Germany), and APPI (Japan) all prohibit moving raw patient data across borders. You have GPU clusters in all 3 countries. Evaluate: (A) train 3 separate models, one per country, (B) federated learning across borders, (C) synthetic data generation to create a de-identified global dataset.

details.common_mistake: Choosing federated learning as an automatic 'best of both worlds' solution. Federated learning still transmits model gradients across borders, which may leak private information through gradient inversion attacks. Regulators may not accept this as compliant.
details.realistic_solution: (A) Separate models: each country trains on local data only. US model is strong (500K records), but Germany (210K) and Japan (125K) models are weak. No compliance risk, but the Japanese model underperforms due to limited data — potentially dangerous in healthcare. (B) Federated learning: trains a shared model by exchanging gradient updates, not raw data. Pros: global model quality with data locality. Cons: gradient inversion attacks can reconstruct training data (recent research shows reconstruction of medical images from gradients). Requires differential privacy noise injection (ε=8) which degrades model quality by ~3-7%. Also, some regulators may argue gradient exchange constitutes 'data transfer.' (C) Synthetic data: generate privacy-safe synthetic records in each country, share synthetic data globally. Pros: clear compliance story (synthetic data is not personal data). Cons: synthetic data quality depends on the generative model; may miss rare disease patterns present in the long tail of real data. Recommendation: Federated learning with differential privacy (ε=4) and secure aggregation as primary approach, with synthetic data augmentation for the data-sparse Japan cohort. This balances model quality, compliance risk, and patient safety.
details.napkin_math: (A) Separate: US accuracy ~94%, Germany ~89%, Japan ~85% (data-limited). (B) Federated: global accuracy ~93% without DP, ~90% with DP (ε=4). Communication: model weights (~500MB) × 100 rounds × 3 sites = 150 GB total cross-border transfer (gradients, not data). (C) Synthetic: depends on generator quality. Best case: ~91%. Worst case: ~86% (misses rare conditions). Federated + DP: highest minimum accuracy across all countries (~90% everywhere vs 85% for Japan standalone).
details.deep_dive_title: Privacy and Compliance in ML Systems

title: The Cable Cut Failover
scenario: Your US-based company serves Asian users via a primary submarine cable route (US West Coast → Japan, 100ms RTT, 80 Tbps capacity). A ship anchor severs the cable (this happens ~100-200 times/year globally). The backup route goes US → Hawaii → Guam → Japan (150ms RTT, 20 Tbps). Your inference service handles 50K requests/second from Asian users. Assess the impact and evaluate failover strategies.

details.common_mistake: Assuming the backup cable handles the same load. The backup route has 4× less capacity and 50% more latency. A naive failover that redirects all traffic to the backup will cause congestion collapse.
details.realistic_solution: Impact assessment: Latency increase: 100ms → 150ms RTT (+50%). TTFT for users: if prefill = 200ms, goes from 300ms to 350ms — may still meet a 500ms SLA. Bandwidth: 80 Tbps → 20 Tbps (4× reduction). At 50K req/s × 10KB average response, current bandwidth need is ~4 Gbps — well within the 20 Tbps backup. But: you share the backup cable with everyone else whose primary was cut. Failover strategies: (1) Active-active multi-path: always use both cables, routing 80% primary / 20% backup. On failure, shift to 100% backup. Latency impact: average RTT increases from 110ms to 150ms. (2) Regional replica: deploy inference capacity in Japan/Singapore. On cable cut, Asian users hit local replicas. Zero latency impact, but costs $200K+/month for idle capacity. (3) Graceful degradation: on cable cut, switch to a smaller/faster model for Asian users (reduce prefill from 200ms to 80ms, compensating for the +50ms RTT), accept slight quality reduction. Recommendation: regional replica for latency-critical services (option 2), with graceful degradation (option 3) as a budget-friendly alternative. The annual expected cost of cable cuts (1-2 incidents × 2-4 weeks repair) justifies the investment.
details.napkin_math: Normal: 100ms RTT + 200ms prefill = 300ms TTFT. Cable cut: 150ms + 200ms = 350ms. SLA (500ms) still met. Bandwidth check: 50K req/s × 10KB = 500MB/s = 4Gbps << 20Tbps backup. Cable repair time: 2-4 weeks. Annual downtime risk: ~2 incidents × 3 weeks = 6 weeks of degraded service. Regional replica cost: $200K/month × 12 = $2.4M/year. Revenue at risk (6 weeks degraded, 10% user churn): depends on business, but often >> $2.4M.

title: The Green AI Paradox
scenario: Your carbon-aware scheduler routes training jobs to the region with the lowest carbon intensity at any given moment. Today it wants to move a 256-GPU training job from Virginia (500 gCO2/kWh, 10ms to training data) to Quebec (20 gCO2/kWh, 80ms to training data). The model trains on a 50TB dataset stored in Virginia. Evaluate whether this migration actually reduces net carbon.

details.common_mistake: Only counting operational carbon (electricity × carbon intensity) and ignoring the carbon cost of data transfer. Moving 50TB of training data across 80ms of network incurs significant energy in networking equipment, and the increased latency may extend training time, consuming more total energy.
details.realistic_solution: Data transfer cost: 50 TB over a 100 Gbps link = 50,000 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 4,000 seconds ≈ 67 minutes. Network energy for transfer: ~0.01 kWh/GB × 50,000 GB = 500 kWh. Transfer carbon at Virginia's rate: 500 × 500 = 250 kg CO2. Training carbon comparison: Training time ≈ 24 hours. Virginia: 256 × 0.7kW × 1.3 PUE × 24h × 500 gCO2/kWh = 2,795 kg CO2. Quebec: same compute + 67min extra startup + potential 10% throughput reduction from data streaming over 80ms link. Energy: 256 × 0.7kW × 1.3 × 26.4h × 20 gCO2/kWh = 123 kg. Total Quebec = 123 + 250 (transfer) = 373 kg. Net savings: 2,795 - 373 = 2,422 kg CO2 (87% reduction). Verdict: the migration IS worth it because the 25× difference in carbon intensity overwhelms the transfer overhead. However, if the carbon intensity difference were only 2×, the transfer cost could negate the savings.
details.napkin_math: Virginia training: 256 × 0.7kW × 1.3 × 24h = 5,591 kWh × 500g = 2,795 kg CO2. Quebec training: 5,591 kWh × 1.1 (throughput penalty) × 20g = 123 kg. Data transfer: 500 kWh × 500g = 250 kg. Quebec total: 373 kg. Savings: 2,422 kg (87%). Break-even carbon ratio: transfer cost / training cost = 250 / 5,591 = 4.5%. Migration not worth it only if Quebec carbon > 500 × (1 - 0.045) ≈ 478 gCO2/kWh.

title: The Inference Placement Decision
scenario: You serve a real-time translation model to users across 40 countries. Option A: one large centralized cluster of 64 H100s in US-East ($2.50/GPU-hour). Option B: 8 small regional clusters of 8 H100s each ($3.20/GPU-hour, 28% premium for regional pricing). Total GPU count is the same (64). The model requires 4 H100s for tensor parallelism. Your P95 latency target is 300ms and inference takes 150ms. Evaluate both architectures.

details.common_mistake: Comparing only the GPU cost and choosing centralized because it is 28% cheaper. The latency analysis reveals that centralized cannot meet the SLA for distant users, which may cost far more in lost users than the 28% savings.
details.realistic_solution: Centralized (US-East): 64 GPUs = 16 inference replicas (4 GPUs each). Latency for nearby users (US/Canada, RTT ~20ms): 20 + 150 = 170ms. PASS. Latency for European users (RTT ~80ms): 80 + 150 = 230ms. PASS. Latency for Asian users (RTT ~150ms): 150 + 150 = 300ms. BORDERLINE. Latency for Australian users (RTT ~200ms): 200 + 150 = 350ms. FAIL. Monthly cost: 64 × $2.50 × 730h = $116,800. Distributed (8 regions): 8 GPUs per region = 2 replicas each. Latency: all users within 30ms of nearest cluster → 30 + 150 = 180ms. All PASS. Monthly cost: 64 × $3.20 × 730h = $149,504. Premium: $32,704/month (28%). But: centralized fails SLA for ~25% of users (Asia-Pacific, Oceania, Africa). If each failed-SLA user represents $0.50/month in revenue and you have 1M users, failing 250K users costs $125K/month — far exceeding the $33K infrastructure premium. Recommendation: Distributed, with the caveat that 2 replicas per region provides minimal redundancy. Consider 6 regions with slightly larger clusters.
details.napkin_math: Centralized cost: 64 × $2.50 × 730 = $116,800/mo. Distributed cost: 64 × $3.20 × 730 = $149,504/mo. Premium: $32,704/mo (28%). SLA compliance: Centralized serves ~75% of users within SLA. Distributed serves ~100%. Revenue risk: 250K users × $0.50 = $125K/mo lost revenue >> $33K infra premium. Distributed wins on total cost of ownership.

title: The GPAI Threshold Debate
scenario: The EU AI Act defines 'General-Purpose AI' (GPAI) models with additional obligations for those trained with more than 10^25 FLOPs (the 'systemic risk' threshold). Your company trained a 70B parameter model using 2,048 H100 GPUs for 90 days at 40% MFU. Does this model exceed the systemic risk threshold? Evaluate the implications for your deployment strategy.

details.common_mistake: Confusing parameter count with training compute. A 70B model does not automatically exceed the threshold — it depends on how long it was trained and at what efficiency. Also, some engineers ignore the threshold entirely, assuming it only applies to companies like OpenAI.
details.realistic_solution: Training compute: H100 peak FP16 = 989 TFLOPS. Effective FLOPS = 989 × 0.40 MFU = 395.6 TFLOPS per GPU. Total compute = 2,048 GPUs × 395.6 TFLOPS × 90 days × 86,400 s/day = 2,048 × 395.6 × 10^12 × 7,776,000 = 6.3 × 10^24 FLOPs. This is BELOW the 10^25 threshold (by ~37%). The model does not trigger systemic risk obligations. However: it is close enough that adding 60% more training (e.g., training for 144 days, or using 3,200 GPUs) would cross the line. Implications: (1) You avoid the most onerous obligations (red-teaming, incident reporting, cybersecurity measures). (2) You still must comply with basic GPAI obligations (technical documentation, copyright compliance, training data summary). (3) Strategic consideration: if you plan to scale up, design your compliance infrastructure now because the next model will likely cross the threshold.

title: The Renewable Intermittency Trap
scenario: A company builds a 50 MW GPU datacenter co-located with a solar farm in Arizona. Solar produces 50 MW for 8 hours/day (capacity factor 33%) and 0 MW at night. Grid backup costs $0.09/kWh vs $0.02/kWh for solar. You run both latency-sensitive inference (30 MW, must run 24/7) and batch training (variable, up to 20 MW, can be deferred). Evaluate power scheduling strategies to minimize cost while maintaining inference availability.

details.common_mistake: Planning to run the entire datacenter on solar and being surprised when 16 hours/day require grid power. Also, treating training and inference identically — inference cannot be deferred, but training can be scheduled to follow solar availability.
details.realistic_solution: Power budget analysis: Inference (24/7): 30 MW × 24h = 720 MWh/day. Training max: 20 MW × 24h = 480 MWh/day. Solar available: 50 MW × 8h = 400 MWh/day. Strategy: During solar hours (8h): Run inference (30 MW from solar) + training at max (20 MW from solar). Total: 50 MW solar, 0 MW grid. Cost: 400 MWh × $0.02 = $8,000. During night (16h): Run inference only (30 MW from grid). No training. Cost: 30 MW × 16h × $0.09 = $43,200. Daily cost: $8,000 + $43,200 = $51,200. Training throughput: 20 MW × 8h = 160 MWh/day (33% of 24/7 max). Alternative (run training 24/7 on grid at night): Night cost with training: (30+20) MW × 16h × $0.09 = $72,000. Daily total: $8,000 + $72,000 = $80,000. Extra cost for 24/7 training: $28,800/day ($10.5M/year) for 67% more training throughput. Decision: depends on whether training speed or cost efficiency is the priority.
details.napkin_math: Solar-only training: Cost = $51,200/day = $18.7M/year. Training capacity: 160 MWh/day. 24/7 training (grid at night): Cost = $80,000/day = $29.2M/year. Training capacity: 480 MWh/day. Cost per training MWh: Solar-schedule: $18.7M / (160 × 365) = $320/MWh. 24/7 with grid: $29.2M / (480 × 365) = $167/MWh. Paradox: running 24/7 is cheaper PER UNIT of training because the inference grid cost is fixed regardless. The marginal cost of night training is only $0.09/kWh = $90/MWh.

title: The Regional Model Routing Decision
scenario: You serve a global chat application. US and EU regions have 8×H100 clusters running a 70B model (300ms TTFT, high quality). Your new Asia-Pacific region only has 2×H100 (budget constraint), which can only run a 13B model (80ms TTFT, lower quality). 40% of your users are in Asia-Pacific. Your PM asks: should all users get the same model, or should Asian users get the smaller, faster model? Evaluate the tradeoffs.

details.common_mistake: Defaulting to 'all users must get the same model for fairness' without quantifying the actual quality-latency tradeoff. Also ignoring that the 70B model served from US to Asia would have 150ms+ RTT added, potentially making the total experience worse than a local 13B model.
details.realistic_solution: Option 1: Same model (70B) for all — route Asian users to US. TTFT for Asian users: 150ms RTT + 300ms prefill = 450ms. Quality: identical to US/EU users. Cost: no additional infra, but cross-Pacific bandwidth costs for 40% of traffic. Option 2: Regional models — 70B in US/EU, 13B in Asia. TTFT for Asian users: 10ms RTT + 80ms prefill = 90ms. Quality: lower (13B vs 70B, roughly 85% as good on benchmarks). Cost: 2×H100 in Asia (~$7K/month). Option 3: Hybrid — route easy queries to 13B locally, hard queries to 70B in US. TTFT: 90ms for easy (70% of queries), 450ms for hard (30%). Average TTFT: 0.7×90 + 0.3×450 = 198ms. Quality: ~95% of always-70B (easy queries are equally good on both models). Recommendation: Option 3 (hybrid routing) — best balance of latency, quality, and cost. The 5× latency improvement for 70% of queries with negligible quality loss is the winning tradeoff. Implement a lightweight query classifier (runs in <5ms) to route between models.
details.napkin_math: Option 1 (centralized 70B): TTFT = 450ms for Asia (40% users). Average global TTFT = 0.6×300 + 0.4×450 = 360ms. Option 2 (regional 13B): TTFT = 90ms for Asia. Average = 0.6×300 + 0.4×90 = 216ms. Quality hit: ~15% for 40% of users. Option 3 (hybrid): TTFT = 198ms for Asia. Average = 0.6×300 + 0.4×198 = 259ms. Quality hit: ~5% for 40% of users (only on the 30% hard queries). Option 3 dominates: best quality-adjusted latency.

title: The Multi-Region Checkpoint Strategy
scenario: You are training a critical 100B parameter model on 512 H100 GPUs in a single datacenter. The checkpoint is 200 GB (FP16 weights + optimizer states). Training takes 30 days. Your datacenter has a 0.1% daily probability of a full outage (power, cooling, or network failure). Evaluate checkpoint strategies: (A) local checkpoints every hour, (B) local every hour + remote replication to another region (100 Gbps link, 40ms RTT) every 6 hours, (C) remote replication every hour.

details.common_mistake: Only considering checkpoint frequency without accounting for the cost of lost work on failure. Also ignoring that remote replication competes for the same network bandwidth used for training communication.
details.realistic_solution: (A) Local only, hourly: Checkpoint write: 200 GB to NVMe at 5 GB/s = 40s. On datacenter failure: ALL progress lost (local storage is in the failed DC). Expected work lost over 30 days: 30 × 0.001 = 3% chance of total loss = 0.03 × 30 = 0.9 expected days lost. But if the DC fails, you lose EVERYTHING — the loss distribution is bimodal (0 or 30 days). (B) Local hourly + remote every 6h: Remote transfer: 200 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 16 seconds. On DC failure, maximum work lost = 6 hours. Expected lost work: 30 × 0.001 × 3h (average) = 0.09 days. Remote transfer bandwidth cost: 16s every 6h = 0.07% of link capacity — negligible. (C) Remote every hour: Transfer: 16s per hour = 0.44% of link capacity. On DC failure, max work lost = 1 hour. Expected: 30 × 0.001 × 0.5h = 0.015 days. Bandwidth impact: minimal. Recommendation: Option C — remote hourly checkpoints. The 16 seconds of network usage per hour is trivial, and reducing max work loss from 'total' to '1 hour' is transformative for a 30-day job. The real cost is storage: 200 GB × 24 checkpoints/day × 30 days = 144 TB of remote checkpoint storage (at $0.023/GB/month on S3 = $3,312/month).
details.napkin_math: (A) Local only: P(failure in 30 days) = 1-(0.999^30) = 2.96%. Expected loss: 0.0296 × 15 days (average) = 0.44 days. But catastrophic risk: 3% chance of losing ALL 30 days. (B) Remote 6h: max loss = 6h. Expected: 0.0296 × 3h = 0.089 days. Storage: 200GB × 4/day × 30 = 24 TB. (C) Remote 1h: max loss = 1h. Expected: 0.0296 × 0.5h = 0.015 days. Storage: 200GB × 24/day × 30 = 144 TB = $3,312/mo. Training compute cost: 512 × $2.50/h × 720h = $921,600. Storage cost is 0.36% of compute — trivially worth it.
